title: Tamezuki and nagashizuki, the two Japanese sheet-formation methods
subtitle: How the Western dip differs from the Japanese flow
excerpt: Japanese papermaking uses two distinct sheet-formation methods: tamezuki, in which the fibres settle by gravity after a single dip, and nagashizuki, in which a formation aid (neri) slows drainage while the maker rocks the mould repeatedly. The two methods produce sheets with very different characteristics.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923), public domain, Internet Archive. Hunter documents both formation methods extensively, including the role of neri in nagashizuki and the regional preferences across Japanese paper-making centres.

glossaryTerms.definition: The Western-influenced Japanese sheet-formation method, analogous to European hand-papermaking: the mould is dipped once and the fibres settle by gravity while the water drains. Produces a thicker, more isotropic sheet than nagashizuki.

A viscous plant extract: traditionally from the tororo-aoi root: added to the vat water in nagashizuki papermaking. Neri slows drainage, allowing the mould to be rocked repeatedly without the fibres settling unevenly.

content.text: Japanese papermaking uses two distinct methods. The first is

: the mould dips once and fibres settle. The second is nagashizuki: a formation aid slows drainage while the mould is rocked. The two methods diverged before the seventeenth century. Each produces paper with different properties.

In tamezuki, the mould is dipped into the vat in a single scoop: identical to European formation. The maker levels the mould, shakes it once or twice to settle the fibres, and waits for the water to drain through the mesh. The fibres settle relatively randomly during the short drain time. The result is a sheet with fibres oriented in multiple directions (isotropic), which makes it strong in all directions and less likely to tear along a grain line.
Tamezuki is used for thicker washi: 50-100 gsm sheets used for wrapping, woodblock printing, and book covers. The sheets drain quickly and are easier to couch and press.

: a viscous extract, traditionally from the tororo-aoi root: to the vat. Neri slows drainage sharply. The maker has time to rock the mould in a lateral motion for 10-20 seconds. Tilting the mould then drains the water. The repeated rocking creates a crossing-fibre structure: layers run in different directions, interlocking as the water drains.

Nagashizuki produces a thin, very strong sheet. The inter-layer fibre crossing gives it exceptional tear resistance. The method is used for tissue-weight washi: 10-30 gsm. At this weight, the crossing fibre orientation is the only structure holding the sheet together. The process takes longer than tamezuki and requires careful neri preparation.
Practical differences for the home papermaker

For cotton-linter sheets at standard weights (80-120 gsm), tamezuki is the natural choice: simpler, faster, no formation aid needed. For kozo-based sheets at tissue weight, nagashizuki with a guar-gum substitute for neri gives a noticeably stronger result than tamezuki would. The choice is not about tradition; it is about what the fibre and the sheet weight require.
